title: Widget Lifecycle

sections.markdown: The widget (State) lifecycle is the sequence of methods Flutter calls on a StatefulWidget's State as it's created, updated and removed.

sections.code:
createState -> initState -> didChangeDependencies -> build
   ... parent rebuilds with new config ...
didUpdateWidget -> build
   ... dependency changes ...
didChangeDependencies -> build
   ... removed ...
deactivate -> dispose

sections.markdown: Forgetting to dispose() AnimationControllers, TextEditingControllers, StreamSubscriptions, or FocusNodes — the #1 source of memory leaks in Flutter. Every controller you create in initState, dispose in dispose().
Never call setState in build() (infinite loop) or after dispose() (throws). Guard async callbacks with `if (mounted)` before calling setState.

flashcards.back: dispose() — controllers, subscriptions, focus nodes, listeners.
When the parent rebuilds this widget with a new configuration.

quiz.explain: initState runs once when the State is inserted.

quiz.q: Failing to dispose a controller causes…
quiz.explain: Undisposed controllers/subscriptions leak memory and resources.

quiz.q: Before setState in an async callback, check…
quiz.explain: The widget may have been disposed; mounted guards against that.

challenges.text: Log every lifecycle method and trigger each transition.
Create and dispose an AnimationController correctly.
Use didUpdateWidget to react to a changed parent parameter.
Reproduce and fix a setState-after-dispose crash with a mounted guard.

interviewQuestions: What goes in initState vs dispose?
When is didUpdateWidget vs didChangeDependencies called?
Why and how do you guard setState with mounted?
Common causes of Flutter memory leaks?
